content.text: A Promise is an object that represents the eventual result of an asynchronous operation. Instead of blocking the program while waiting for a network request or a timer to finish, a Promise lets you attach callbacks that run when the result is ready. Every Promise is in exactly one of three states:
(the operation succeeded and produced a value), or
(the operation failed with a reason). Once a Promise leaves the pending state it is settled permanently — it cannot change again.
transitions the Promise to fulfilled; calling
transitions it to rejected. Calling one after the other has no effect — only the first call matters.

fetch('https://api.example.com/user/1')
  .then(response => response.json())   // runs when fetch succeeds
  .then(user => console.log(user))     // runs with the parsed JSON
  .catch(error => console.error(error)) // runs if any step rejects
  .finally(() => console.log('done')); // always runs
Each method returns a new Promise, which is what makes chaining possible:
— called with the resolved value. Whatever
returns becomes the value of the next Promise in the chain.
— called with the rejection reason. Equivalent to
. Errors propagate down the chain to the nearest
— runs regardless of outcome. Useful for cleanup (hiding a spinner, releasing a lock). It receives no value and does not affect the resolved value passed downstream.
returns a new Promise, you can sequence asynchronous steps without nesting callbacks:
returns a plain value, the next
receives it directly. If it returns another Promise, the chain pauses until that inner Promise settles. If it throws, the chain skips to the nearest
These static methods create immediately-settled Promises. They are useful in tests, default fallbacks, and when an API must return a Promise but the value is already known:

The most common place for Promises in React is inside
when fetching data on mount. The effect callback itself cannot be
(it must return either nothing or a cleanup function, not a Promise), so you call
directly or define an async function inside and call it immediately.
flag prevents a state update on an already-unmounted component — a common source of the "Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component" warning. An
is a cleaner alternative for fetch-based requests and actually cancels the network request, not just the state update.
A Promise has three states — pending, fulfilled, rejected — and transitions only once. It is the standard way JavaScript represents deferred asynchronous values.
handles a fulfilled value and returns a new Promise;
returns a new Promise. Returning a Promise from inside
causes the chain to wait for it; throwing causes the chain to skip to
to any Promise chain. Unhandled rejections produce warnings in browsers and can crash Node.js processes.
for data fetching. Guard state updates with a cancellation flag (or
) so the component does not update after unmounting.
